text: Supplementary Records of the Supernatural** Compiled by **Tao Qian of the Jin Dynasty **Chapter One** Ding Lingwei, originally from Liaodong, studied Daoist cultivation on Lingxu Mountain. Later, he transformed into a crane and returned to Liaodong, where he alighted on the ornamental pillars of the city gate. At that time, a young man raised his bow to shoot the crane. The crane then flew, circling in the air and speaking: "There is a bird, a bird named Ding Lingwei. After a thousand years, I return home. The city remains the same, but the people have changed. Why did you not learn the Dao and become an immortal, leaving behind only grave mounds?" The crane then soared high into the sky. Today, the people of Liaodong say that their ancestors had ascended to immortality, but they do not know the names of these immortals. To the north of Songshan Mountain, there is a large cave whose depth cannot be measured. Common people visit and observe it during festivals. In the early Jin dynasty, there was once a man who accidentally fell into the cave. His companions hoped he might not die and threw food into the cave. The man who fell found the food and began to explore the cave. After about ten days, he suddenly saw light. He then came upon a small thatched house, where two people were sitting across from each other playing chess. On the chessboard lay a cup of white liquid. The man who fell told them of his hunger and thirst. One of the chess players said, "You may drink this." He drank it, and his strength increased tenfold. The chess player asked, "Do you wish to stay here?" The man did not want to stay. The chess player said, "Continue to the west, where there is a natural well filled with many water dragons. Simply throw yourself into the well, and you will emerge. If you are hungry, take something from the well to eat." The man followed their instructions and, after about six months, emerged in Sichuan. He returned to Luoyang and asked Zhang Hua about his experience. Zhang Hua said, "These were officials of the celestial bureaucracy. The drink you consumed was jade essence, and the food you ate was the stone marrow from a dragon cave."

In Yandian County of Kuaiji, there were two men named Yuan Xiang and Gen Shuo who went hunting. They traversed many deep mountains and ridges, and saw a group of six or seven wild goats. They chased the goats and crossed a narrow and steep stone bridge. As the goats ran, Gen and his companions followed, heading towards a sheer cliff. The cliff was bright red and stood upright, known as the Red City. Above it, water flowed down, wide and narrow like a piece of cloth, which the people of Yandian called a waterfall. There was a mountain cave like a door, and they passed through it suddenly. Inside, it was very flat and spacious, with fragrant grass and trees. There was a small house where two young women lived, both around fifteen or sixteen years old, with beautiful appearances, dressed in green clothes. One was named Yingzhu, and the other was named Jieyu. When the two men arrived, the women happily said, "We have been expecting you." They then became husband and wife. Suddenly, the two women went out, saying that someone else had also found a husband and went to congratulate them. They walked on the sheer cliff, making a clear and resonant sound. The two men wanted to return home, secretly leaving the way back. The two women already knew, and they chased them back, saying, "You can go." They then gave a small bag to Gen and his companions, telling them, "Be careful not to open it." So they returned home. Later, when they went out, their family opened the bag to look inside. The bag was like a lotus, with one layer after another, up to five layers, and inside was a small green bird that flew away. Gen returned and knew this, feeling sad. Later, when Gen was working in the fields, his family brought him food as usual, but he was seen standing still in the field. They approached to see, and there was only a shell like a cicada's molt.

A man from Xingyang, whose name is forgotten, was a well-known scholar. The Governor of Jingzhou appointed him as a vice governor, but he refused the appointment and retreated to seclude himself and cultivate his aspirations. One day, he went to a farm where people were harvesting their crops on the field. Suddenly, a person appeared, over a zhang tall, wearing a loose, simple robe and a headband, and came towards him. Gracefully raising both hands, the person danced towards him and said, "Have you ever seen the 'Shao Dance'? This is the 'Shao Dance.'" Dancing and leaving, the person departed. He followed, directly towards a mountain. The mountain had a cave, just big enough for one person. The person immediately entered the cave, and he also followed in. At first, it was very narrow, then it became spacious, and he lost sight of the person. He saw several hundred acres of good land. He then cultivated it as his family's legacy, and his descendants still rely on it.

During the reign of Emperor Hui of Jin, a man from Wuling made a living by fishing. Following the stream, he forgot how far he had traveled, and suddenly came upon a peach blossom forest. On both banks for several hundred steps, there were no mixed trees, only beautiful flowers and falling petals. The fisherman found it very strange. He went forward again, wanting to explore the entire forest. The forest ended at the water source, and he found a mountain. There was a small opening in the mountain, as if there was light inside. He left his boat and entered through the opening. It was very narrow at first, barely wide enough for a person to pass through. After walking several dozen steps, it suddenly opened up into an open space. The land was open, and the houses were neatly arranged. There were good fields, beautiful ponds, mulberry trees, and bamboo. The paths were well-connected, and the sounds of chickens and dogs were heard. The men and women were dressed just like those outside. The elderly and children were all happy and content. They saw the fisherman and were greatly surprised, asking where he came from, and he answered them in detail. They invited him to return home, preparing wine, killing a chicken, and cooking food for him. The villagers heard about this person and all came to inquire. They said their ancestors fled from the Qin troubles, leading their wives, children, and fellow townspeople to this isolated place, never leaving again, thus being cut off from the outside world. They asked what era it was now, and they did not know about the Han dynasty, let alone the Wei and Jin dynasties. He told them everything he had heard, and they all sighed in amazement. The others also invited him to their homes, all offering wine and food. He stayed for several days before taking his leave. The people here told him, "It is not worth telling outsiders about." After leaving, he found his boat, retraced his steps, and marked the path everywhere. When he reached the county, he went to the governor and told him about it. Governor Liu Xun immediately sent people to follow him, searching for the marks he had made, but they could not find the way again.

Liu Ziji of Nanyang, also known as Ziji, enjoyed traveling and exploring mountains and rivers. Once, while gathering herbs on Mount Heng, he delved so deeply that he forgot to return. He saw a stream, with two stone caves on the south side, one closed and one open. The stream was deep and wide, making it impossible to cross. Intending to return, he lost his way and encountered a woodcutter. He asked for directions and barely managed to return home. Someone claimed that the caves contained magical formulas, elixirs, and various objects. Liu Ziji wanted to search again but could no longer find the location. In Liling County of Changsha, there was a small stream. Two people were rowing a boat to gather firewood when they saw water flowing out of a hole in the bank, with freshly cut wood pieces floating downstream. They were surprised to find human footprints in the deep mountains. They said to each other, "You can try entering the water to see what it is like." One of them put on a hat to cover himself and entered the cave, which was just big enough for a person. After walking several dozen steps, he found himself in an open and bright space, yet it was no different from the world outside. In Pingle County, there is a mountain overlooking water, with rock formations resembling eyes, large and clear, with distinct white and black pupils, known as "Eye Rock." On Jishan Mountain in Shixing, there are two rock formations facing each other like the tail of a mythical bird. There are dozens of stone chambers. Those who pass by all hear the sounds of metal, stone, strings, and bamboo.

In Zhongxu County, there is a Chaste Maiden Gorge. On the western bank of the gorge, near the water's edge, there is a stone that resembles a human shadow, shaped like a woman. This is called "Chaste Maiden." According to local elders, during the Qin dynasty, several girls came here to gather abalone. During a stormy day with heavy rain and darkness, one girl transformed into this stone. Forty li south of Lincheng County, there is Gaishan Mountain, and about a hundred steps away is the Gushu Spring. Once, a girl named Shu was gathering firewood with her father at this spring. The girl sat down, and no matter how hard she was pulled, she could not be moved. She then returned home to inform her family. By the time she got back, she only saw serene and still clear spring water. Her mother said, "My daughter loves music." So, they played stringed instruments and sang, causing the spring water to surge and flow in circles, and a pair of red carp appeared. Now, when people play music and engage in recreational activities, the spring water surges out. In Volume Two: Wu Meng, a scholar-official with magical arts, was invited by his fellow countyman Zou Huizheng. At night, they burned incense in the courtyard. Suddenly, a tiger carried away Zou's child over the fence. Wu Meng said, "There is no harm, he will return shortly." The tiger went several dozen steps and then suddenly returned the child. Zou then became more diligent and asked to become a good Taoist. Wu Meng was extremely filial. When he was a small child, he slept beside his parents during the summer when there were many mosquitoes, but he never fanned himself. His fellow sleeper noticed and asked why, and he replied, "I fear that the mosquitoes will bite my parents." When his parents passed away, he lay by their graves. During a bandit raid, the bandits committed acts of violence, burned houses, and excavated graves. The people fled. Wu Meng lay by the grave and cried bitterly, not leaving. The bandits were moved to sorrow and did not attack.

Xie Yun returned from Mount Wudang and was seated with Huan Xuanwu. Someone mentioned that Zuo Yuanfang had provided perch to Cao Cao, and Xie Yun remarked, "This is possible." He then asked for a large jar to hold water, wrote a red talisman, and threw it into the water. Soon, a carp began flapping its fins in the water. In Qiantang, there was a man named Du Zigong who possessed secret techniques. Once, he borrowed a melon knife from someone, and the owner asked for its return. Du Zigong said, "I will return it immediately." Later, the owner of the knife traveled to Jiaxing and found a fish jump into his boat. When he opened the fish's belly, he discovered the melon knife inside. During the Tai Xing period, Qu Chun of Hengyang created a mouse market: it was about four zhang square, with four doors, each having a wooden figure. He released four or five mice inside, and if they tried to leave, the wooden figures would push them back. Duke Huan Wen of Jin, courtesy name Yuanzi, in his later years, suddenly encountered a nun whose name was unknown, from a distant place. She offered herself to Huan Wen as a patron. The nun's conduct was not constant, and she resented his respectful treatment, living in his inner quarters. Whenever she bathed, she would do so for a long time. Huan Wen suspected and peeked. He saw the nun naked, swinging a knife, opening her belly, removing her organs, cutting off her head and body, slicing them into pieces. Huan Wen was startled and returned. When the nun emerged from the bath, her body was as usual. Huan Wen asked her about the truth, and the nun replied, "If I were to harm your lord, my body would be like this." At that time, Huan Wen was plotting to seize power, and hearing this, he was greatly disappointed. Therefore, he was warned and ultimately maintained his loyalty as a minister. The nun later left, and her whereabouts are unknown.

In the state of Pei there lived a scholar named Zhou, who had three sons born at the same time. When they were about to come of age, they all had voices but could not speak. One day, a guest passed by the door and asked for a drink. Hearing the sound of the children, he asked, “What is this sound?” Zhou replied, “These are my sons, all of whom cannot speak.” The guest said, “You should look inward and examine your faults—how did this come to be?” The host found his words strange and realized he was no ordinary man. After a long while, he came out and said, “I do not remember any wrongdoing.” The guest said, “Try to think again about your childhood.” He went inside and, after the time it takes to eat a meal, came out and said to the guest, “I remember when I was a child. There was a swallow’s nest on the bed, with three young birds inside. Their mother would come from outside to bring food, and all three would open their mouths to receive it, day after day. I once tried putting my finger into the nest, and the young swallows also opened their mouths to take it. Then I took three plants—probably a kind of bramble or thorny bush—and gave them to each of the young swallows to eat. Soon after, they all died. When the mother returned and saw her young gone, she cried bitterly and flew away. There was once a similar event in the past; now I truly regret it.” When the guest heard this, he transformed into the appearance of a Daoist monk and said, “Since you have come to know your own remorse, your sins are now forgiven.” After he spoke these words, the host suddenly heard his sons speaking. Zhou also suddenly saw no trace of the Daoist monk anymore.

A Person from Tianzhu Named Fotu Cheng In the fourth year of Yongjia (310 AD), a person from Tianzhu named Fotu Cheng came to Luoyang. He was skilled in reciting divine incantations and could command spirits and deities. He had a hole beside his abdomen, which he often plugged with cotton. Every night, when he read books, he would remove the cotton, and light would shine out from the hole, illuminating the entire room. At dawn, he would go to the side of a flowing stream, pull out his five viscera and six bowels through the hole, wash them, and then put them back inside his abdomen. In Ye, there was a foreign Taoist priest named Shi Hu, who knew spells and incantations. He rode a donkey and acted as a merchant, traveling through the deep mountains of a foreign land. Below was a deep ravine with no bottom. Suddenly, a wicked ghost stole the priest's donkey and led it down into the deep ravine. The priest followed the trail, recited spells, and summoned the kings of ghosts. In a short while, the donkey and belongings were restored to their original state. Tan You, a Taoist priest, lived a life of austerity as a monk. In Shanyang County, there was a family that practiced witchcraft. Anyone who ate their food would die by vomiting blood. Tan You once visited them. The host placed food on the table, and Tan You followed his usual ritual and made a wish. Two centipedes, each over a foot long, appeared and jumped around on the table. Tan You then ate his fill and returned home safely, without any harm. In Gao Kui's house, there were ghosts and strange phenomena. They spoke harshly, threw things around, and their forms were not visible. Sometimes objects would move on their own and repeatedly catch fire. Wizards and shamans tried to exorcise them but could not stop them. Just then, a fortunate spirit arrived, and he invited it. He went to the door and saw many talismans, which he all burned. He sat on a small platform and left. That night, the ghosts and strange phenomena ceased.

Zhao Gu often rode a red horse in battle, greatly loving and valuing it. He usually tethered the horse in front of his residence, but one day the horse suddenly bloated and died shortly after. Guo Pu passed by from the north and went to visit Zhao Gu. The gatekeeper said, "General Zhao's horse was very well cared for. Now that it has died, he is very upset." Guo Pu then told the gatekeeper, "Tell Zhao Gu that I can bring the horse back to life, and he will certainly see me." The gatekeeper, hearing this, was surprised and delighted, and immediately informed Zhao Gu. Zhao Gu was overjoyed and ordered the gatekeeper to go fetch Guo Pu. When they first met and exchanged pleasantries, Zhao Gu asked, "Can you bring my horse back to life?" Guo Pu replied, "I can bring it back to life." Zhao Gu was delighted and asked, "What kind of method do you need?" Guo Pu said, "Get about twenty or thirty of your loyal and strong men, all to hold bamboo poles, and travel east for thirty miles from here. There will be a hill, forest, and trees, resembling a temple. If you find such a place, use the bamboo poles to stir and hit it. You will find an object, and you should quickly bring it back. Once you get this object, the horse will come back to life." Zhao Gu then sent fifty brave and valiant men to go. Just as Guo Pu had said, they found a large grove and saw an object that looked like a monkey and could fly and run. The brave men chased it down and brought it back. This object, seeing the dead horse from afar, jumped and wanted to go there. Guo Pu ordered to let it go. This object then went to the horse's head and breathed on its nose. After a long time, the horse stood up, snorted, and ran quickly, and then the object disappeared. Zhao Gu generously rewarded Guo Pu, who then crossed the river to the east.

Wang Xianmin once ordered Guo Pu to divinate the auspiciousness or inauspiciousness of his own year. Guo Pu said, "There will be a small misfortune. You can take two large jars from Guangzhou, fill them with water, place them on the bed with two corners, name them 'Mirror Good,' and ward off it. At a certain time, remove the jars and the water." Wang Xianmin then found a lost copper mirror, but did not know its location. Later, when he removed the water, he saw the lost mirror inside the jar. The mouth of the jar was several inches, and the mirror was more than a foot in size. He then ordered Guo Pu to divinate the meaning of the mirror and the jar. Guo Pu said, "Removing the jars was delayed, thus causing this misfortune. It was caused by evil spirits, no other reason." He had the vehicle's axle burned, and the mirror immediately appeared. In the early period of the Eastern Han Dynasty, Guo Pu often divined for himself and knew his fate would be disastrous. Once passing by Jiankang Gate and Pond, he met a young man who hurriedly walked, very cold. He pulled him, took off his silk robe, and gave it to him. The man refused to take it, and Guo Pu said, "Just take it, you will know later." When he was about to die, it was indeed this person who executed him. Others all tried to plead for him, and Guo Pu said, "I have entrusted him for a long time." This person was choked with sobs. After the execution was completed, this person then explained.

Xi Chao, a man from Gaoping, was in his twenties and suffered from a serious illness. Du Buqian, a man from Lüjiang, had learned the *I Ching* and divination from his maternal grandfather, Guo Pu, and had considerable experience. Chao ordered him to try divination. When the hexagram was cast, Buqian said, "According to the hexagram, your illness will soon be cured. However, you should go to a family named Shanggong, thirty li northeast, and look for the male pheasant they are raising. Put it in a cage and tie it up, and place it under the eastern eaves. Nine days later, on the day of the double fifth at midday, there will definitely be a wild female pheasant flying over. It will mate with it. After mating, they will fly away together. If this happens, your illness will be completely cured within twenty days. This is also a good omen. You will live to be eighty years old and reach the highest rank of a minister. If only the female flies away and the male stays, your illness will take a full year to recover. You will live to be half of eighty years old and lose your position and reputation." At the time, Chao was extremely weak and feared his life was in danger. He laughed and replied, "If I can live to half of eighty, that would be enough. A full year of illness, what is worth waiting for?" However, he did not believe it. Someone advised him to follow the advice and look for the male pheasant, and he indeed found it. On the day of the double fifth, Chao lay under the southern veranda to watch. Until dusk, there was indeed a male pheasant that flew into the cage and mated with the male pheasant before flying away. The male pheasant did not move. Chao sighed and said, "The wonders of Guan and Guo, how can they surpass this!" His illness lasted more than a year before he recovered, and he lived to be forty years old, dying as a Lang of the Central Secretariat.

#### Chapter Three **Cheng Xian, courtesy name Xianxiu.** His mother was pregnant with him when she dreamed of an old man giving her medicine and said, "Take this, and you will bear a noble son." During the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, Cheng Xian rose through the ranks to become a Shizhong (Attendant-in-Waiting) and gained fame in the world. **Yuan Zhen was in Yu Prefecture.** He sent a female entertainer named Jiling to deliver three courtesans—Xue, Guo, and Ma—to Huan Xuanwu. After they arrived, after some time, the three women came out at midnight to gaze at the moon in the courtyard. There was a bronze jar of water beside them. Suddenly, they saw a shooting star fall directly into the jar from the sky. They were surprised and looked at it together. Suddenly, it appeared as a small, glowing pearl, flowing to the bottom of the water, shining brightly and clearly. They then said to each other, "This is a sign of good fortune. Who will it benefit?" Xue and Guo tried to catch it with a ladle and a bowl, but failed. Ma was the last to try, and the star fell right into her ladle. She drank it immediately. Soon after, she felt something and soon became pregnant with Huan Xuan. Although Huan Xuan eventually seized power but did not maintain it, he enjoyed great honor and wealth for several years. **Linhuai Gong Xun Xu, courtesy name Xiuxuan.** His mother, Lady Hua, loved him excessively. When he was ten years old, he traveled south with his family and passed through Qingcao Lake. At the time, the wind was strong and the sails were full. Xu suddenly fell into the water while leaving the city gate. By the time they lowered the sails, they had already traveled dozens of miles. The waters were vast and turbulent, and his mother touched her chest and looked far away. After a short while, she saw a small boat with a fisherman rowing it like a bird. The fisherman brought Xu back to safety and said, "I am returning the young master to you." Later, Xun Xu rose to the positions of Changbo (a high-ranking official) and Governor of Changsha, hence the term "young master" (府君).

Wen Chao of Baqiu in Luzhou, through generations, made farming his occupation. Annually, he cultivated dozens of hectares of land, and the family gradually became wealthy. At the beginning of the Taiyuan era of the Jin dynasty, after the autumn harvest, all the harvesting was completed. The next morning, he went to the fields, and the rice was fully restored, as clear and unchanged as before. Immediately, he harvested again, and the harvest filled the granary. Thus, he became very wealthy. Wei Quan of Shangyu lived in the northern part of the county. Suddenly, there was a person wearing mourning clothes, a black hat, and a handkerchief covering his mouth, who came to the entire family. He said, "You have one million coins, and copper objects are also like this. There is a large willow tree with money underneath it. If you take the money, you will get it. It is very unlucky for your family. I will soon take this for you." Then he left. Since then, for thirty years, he did not come back. The whole family also did not take the money. In the first year of the Yuangang era of the Liu Song dynasty, more than a hundred bandits from Jian'an County attacked and destroyed the county seat, raided the people's property and children, and then entered the Buddhist temple, searching for and looting treasures. Before that, all the offerings were separately sealed and placed in a room. When the bandits broke into the house, suddenly, there were tens of thousands of bees that came out of the clothes chest, bit and stung simultaneously. The bandits' bodies and heads swelled and hurt, and their eyes all became blind and closed. They abandoned all the things they had plundered and fled.

Cai Yi was courageous, his voice as loud as thunder. Once, two thieves entered his room, and Cai Yi clapped the bed and shouted once, causing both thieves to fall down. Once there was a man who, along with his servant, contracted a disease called 腹瘕 (abdominal tumor). The servant died, and the man then cut open his abdomen to examine it. He found a white turtle with red eyes, very bright and vivid. He tried to pour various toxic medicines over it, and even put medicine in the turtle's mouth, but it showed no damage or reaction. He then tied the turtle to the bedpost. Suddenly, a guest came to visit, riding a white horse. The horse's urine splashed on the turtle, which became frightened, wanting to quickly run away to avoid the urine. Being tied, it could not move, so it retracted its head, neck, and feet. The patient observed this and said to his son, "My illness might be curable." He then tried to pour the white horse's urine over the turtle, and in a short while, it dissolved into several liters of water. The patient then drank several liters of the white horse's urine, and the illness suddenly healed. High-ranking official Xishi (Xie Jian), stationed in Dantu, once went hunting in early February when ferns began to grow. A soldier broke off and ate one stem, immediately feeling a slight nausea in his heart, wanting to vomit. He returned home, and then developed abdominal pain. After about half a year, he suddenly vomited, expelling a red snake over a foot long, still moving. He hung it on the eaves, and the snake gradually dried and shrank. After one night, it was just a stem of fern, the same one he had eaten. The illness was then cured.

During the reign of Huan Wen, there was a military officer who, after suffering from a lingering fever, could drink even more tea. He would have to consume one hu and two dou to feel full. If the amount was reduced even slightly, he would feel it was insufficient. This was not a one-time occurrence. His family was poor. Later, a guest visited him and found him drinking tea. The guest had heard of this condition before and ordered him to drink five sheng more. The officer then vomited heavily, expelling an object about the size of a sheng, with a mouth, a shrunken and wrinkled form, and a shape resembling a cow's stomach. The guest placed the object in a basin and poured one hu and two dou of tea over it. The object absorbed all the tea and only felt slightly bloated. When another five sheng of tea was added, the object came out in a mixed state from his mouth. After expelling the object, the officer's illness was cured. Someone asked him, "What was this illness?" He replied, "It is called 'Hu'er Jia.'" Huan Zhe, courtesy name Mingqi, lived in Yuzhang when Mei Yuanlong was the governor. Mei had already fallen ill, and Huan went to visit him. He told Mei, "Last night, I suddenly dreamed of being a servant, welcoming you to become the Lord of Mount Tai." Mei was startled and said, "I also dreamed that you were a servant, wearing mourning clothes, coming to welcome me." After several days, they had the same dream again, and it was said that on the 28th day, he would be enfeoffed. On the 27th day at dusk, Huan suddenly fell ill with a full abdomen and asked Mei for musk pills. Upon hearing this, Mei ordered the preparation of funeral items. On the 27th day, Huan died; on the 28th day, Mei also passed away.

Hua Xun of Pingyuan, courtesy name Zi Yu, was a student when he often stayed outside someone's door. One night, the host's wife gave birth. Shortly after, two officials came to his door, immediately stepping back and hesitating before saying to each other, "He is here." They then lingered for a long time. One official said, "The records should be settled, why stay here?" They then came forward to bow to Zi Yu and led him inside. When they went out, they walked together and said, "How many years will it be?" One of them replied, "It will be three years." When it was light, Zi Yu left. Later, he wanted to verify the matter and went after three years to check on the child's condition, and indeed, the child had died after three years. He then said to himself, "I was indeed right." Later, he became Grand Commandant. During the Song dynasty, there was a person whose surname was forgotten, and he slept with his wife. When it was light, the wife got up and went out. Later, his husband also went out. The wife returned and saw her husband still sleeping in the bed. Soon, a servant came from outside and said, "The master is looking for a mirror." The wife thought the servant was lying and pointed to the bed to show him. The servant said, "I just came from the master's side." She then hurriedly told her husband. The husband was greatly surprised and went in. He and his wife looked together and saw the person in the bed sleeping comfortably with a high pillow, exactly his form, with no difference at all. They thought it might be his spirit and dared not disturb it. They then slowly touched the bed with their hands, and it gradually disappeared into the bed. The couple were terrified and could not stop. After a while, the husband suddenly fell ill, his mental state becoming confused, and he never recovered.

Dong Shouzhi was executed, but his family had not yet heard the news. His wife was sitting at night when she suddenly saw Dong Shouzhi seated beside her, sighing incessantly. She asked, "How did you manage to return at night?" Dong Shouzhi did not respond at all. After a while, he went out and walked around the chicken coop, causing the chickens in the cage to scream in fright. Suspecting something strange, the wife took a torch and went out to look, seeing several liters of blood, and Dong Shouzhi had disappeared. She then told her mother-in-law, and they all cried together, realizing that something had happened. By morning, they indeed received the bad news. During the Song dynasty, there was a scholar studying far away. His parents lit a fire and worked at night when the child suddenly appeared in front of them, sighing and saying, "Now I am just a spirit, not a living person anymore." The parents asked him, and he replied, "I fell ill this month and died at a certain time today. I am now at the home of Ren Zicheng in Langya, and I will be buried tomorrow. Come to fetch my parents." The parents said, "It's a thousand miles away, even if we rush, how can we reach you?" The child said, "There are carriages outside, just ride them, and you will arrive." The parents followed his advice, got on the carriage and seemed to fall asleep. By the time the rooster crowed, they had already arrived. They saw the carriages and horses, which were just made of wood. They then met the owner, grieved over their child, and asked about the child's illness and news, as he had said.

Chapter Four During the Jin Dynasty, Feng Xiaojiang of Dongping was appointed as the governor of Guangzhou. He had a son named Ma Zi, who was in his twenties and often slept alone in the stable. One night, Ma Zi dreamed of a woman in her late teens who said, "I am the daughter of the former governor of Beihai, Xu Xuanfang. Unfortunately, I died young. It has been four years since my death, and I was unjustly killed by spirits. According to the records of life, I should live to be over eighty. If you agree to help me, I can be reborn, and I must rely on Ma Zi to live. I should also become your wife. Can you save me?" Ma Zi replied, "I can do that." They agreed on a specific date for her to appear. On that date, Ma Zi noticed that the ground in front of his bed had hair just level with it. When he swept it away, it became even clearer, and he realized it was the woman from his dream. He then dismissed everyone around him, and she slowly began to appear from his forehead, then her head and face, and finally her shoulders, neck, and body. Ma Zi made her sit opposite him on the bed, and they arranged their conversation, which was extraordinarily wonderful. They then slept together. She often warned him, saying, "I am still incomplete."

Someone asked when she would be released, and she replied, "She will be released on her birthday in her birth year, but it has not yet arrived." They then went to the stable, where their voices were audible to everyone. The woman calculated that her birthday had arrived, and then she taught the man the method for her own care. After speaking, she left. The man followed her instructions and, on the designated day, prepared a red rooster, a tray of millet rice, a cup of clear wine, and placed them in front of the grave, about ten steps away from the stable. After the ritual was completed, they dug up the coffin and opened it. The woman's body appeared unchanged. They gently carried her out and placed her in a felt tent. Her heart was slightly warm, and she had a faint breath. They arranged for four maids to care for her. They regularly dripped the milk of a green sheep into her eyes, and gradually, her eyes opened, she could swallow porridge, and then she could speak. After two hundred days, she could walk with a cane, and after a year, her complexion, skin, and strength had fully returned to normal. They then sent word to the Xu family, and everyone gathered. A favorable date was chosen, and they were formally engaged. They had two sons and a daughter: the eldest son, named Yuanqing, served as a Secretary of the Secretariat in the early Yongjia period; the younger son, named Jingdu, became an Assistant to the Grand Tutor; and the daughter married Liu Ziyi of Jinan, the grandson of the retired scholar Yan Shi.

Gan Bao, courtesy name Lingsheng, was originally from Xincai. His father, Ying, had a favored concubine. His mother was extremely jealous. When Gan Bao's father was buried, because the concubine was still alive, they pushed the maid into the tomb. Gan Bao's brothers were young and did not know about it. After ten years, when his mother died, they opened the tomb and saw the concubine lying on the coffin, her clothes as if she were alive. When they looked at her, she was still warm and gradually had breath. They carried her back home, and she revived after a whole day. She said that Gan Bao's father often brought her food and drink, had sexual relations with her, and their affection was as if she were alive. Family matters of good or bad fortune, she always told him, and checking confirmed that everything was true. After several years of recovery, she finally died. Gan Bao's brother once had a near-death illness and was not cold for several days. Later, he woke up and said he saw ghosts and spirits in the world, like waking from a dream, and did not know he was dead.

During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, a man from Beidi named Chen Liang was friends with Liu Shu from Pei and also engaged in commerce with his fellow countyman Li Yan. After they made a significant profit, Li Yan killed Chen Liang to take his possessions. Ten or so days later, Chen Liang suddenly revived and returned home. He recounted that when he died, he saw his friend Liu Shu, who had long since passed away. Liu Shu told him, "Last year during the spring sacrifice ceremony, there was a quarrel in my family, and I was truly angry. I made a horned beast in front of the courtyard. When you return, can you tell me about this?" Chen Liang then went to report this to Liu Shu's family, and the strange occurrences ceased. He then went to the authorities to report Li Yan and admit his crime. In Xiangyang, a man named Li Chú died from an illness. His father guarded his body. Until the third watch of the night, Li Chú suddenly sat up and grabbed his wife's gold bracelet with great urgency. His father helped him take it off. After holding it, he died again. His wife observed him, and by morning, his heart felt warmer, and he slowly revived. He said, "Officials took me away, and there were many others like me. I saw someone who paid a bribe and was released. So I agreed to give the official the gold bracelet. The official told me to return home to get it and gave it to me. After getting the bracelet, the official let me return. I saw the official take the bracelet away." Several days later, he didn't know it was still in his wife's clothes. His wife dared not wear it again and buried it according to the instructions.

Zheng Mao died of illness, and after the funeral rites were completed, he was not yet buried. Suddenly, his wife and family members dreamed that Zheng Mao said, "I was not supposed to die; it was just a temporary fainting spell. You can open the coffin and take me out, and use the heated carriage pot to warm my head." Following his words, he came back to life. During the Jin dynasty, Li Zhongwen, the governor of Wudu, lost his daughter in the capital when she was eighteen years old. He temporarily buried her north of the city. There was Zhang Shizhi who succeeded him as the governor. Zhang Shizhi's son, named Zichang, was twenty years old. One night, while he was serving in the stable, he dreamed of a woman, about seventeen or eighteen years old, whose appearance was unusual. She said, "I was the daughter of the previous governor, unfortunately, I died young. Now I will be reborn. I love you and am happy, so I came to be with you." This happened for five or six nights. Suddenly, she appeared during the day, her clothes fragrant and exceptionally beautiful. They became husband and wife, and her clothes were stained, just like a virgin. Later, Li Zhongwen sent a maid to check on his daughter's grave, and she passed by Zhang Shizhi's wife. She entered the stable and saw one of the girl's shoes under Zichang's bed. She took it, cried, and called out to dig up the grave. She brought the shoe back and showed it to Li Zhongwen. Li Zhongwen was shocked and asked Zhang Shizhi, "Can your son have gotten my daughter's shoe?" Zhang Shizhi called his son and asked him about it. The son explained the whole story. Both Li and Zhang found it strange. They opened the coffin and found that the girl's body had grown flesh, her appearance unchanged, with a shoe on her right foot but not on the left. After that, she died again, and the flesh rotted, and she could not be revived. With countless regrets, what more can I say! They parted with tears.

During the Wei dynasty, in the northern mountains of Xunyang County, a barbarian had a skill that could transform people into tigers. The fur, claws, and teeth of the transformed individuals were all like those of a real tiger. A local man named Zhou had a servant who was sent to cut wood in the mountains. The servant's wife and sister accompanied him. Upon arriving in the mountains, the servant told the two women, "You should climb a tall tree and watch what I do." They followed his advice. Soon after, he entered the grass. After a while, they saw a large yellow-spotted tiger emerge from the grass, roaring fiercely, which was very terrifying. The two women were greatly frightened. After a long time, the tiger returned to the grass, and soon after, it reappeared as a human. The transformed servant said to the two women, "Be careful not to mention it when you return home." Later, the two women told others about the incident. Zhou found out and gave the servant strong wine to drink until he was drunk. He then had the servant's clothes and body examined, and everything was detailed and there was no other difference. Only in the hair bun, he found a piece of paper, drawn as a large tiger with a talisman by its side. When the servant woke up, Zhou called him and asked what happened. Seeing that the secret was out, the servant told the whole story, saying, "I once bought grain from the barbarians. One of the barbarian masters told me about this skill and gave me three feet of cloth, several sheng of rice, one red rooster, and one sheng of wine, with which I performed the transformation."

During the reign of Emperor An of Jin, there was a man named Xie Duan from Houguan who, at a young age, lost his parents and had no relatives. He was raised by his neighbors. By the time he was seventeen or eighteen, he was respectful and cautious, never engaging in illegal activities. When he began living independently, he had not yet married. His neighbors, feeling compassion for him, planned to arrange a marriage for him, but it did not come to fruition. Xie Duan worked tirelessly, rising early and retiring late, tending to his fields without rest. One day, while in the village, he found a large snail, about the size of a three-sheng pot. Thinking it was a strange object, he took it home and placed it in a jar. He kept it for several days. Each morning, when he returned from the fields, he would find food and drink prepared in his house, as if someone else had done it. He assumed his neighbor was doing him a favor. This happened repeatedly, so he went to thank his neighbor. The neighbor said, “I did not do this; why are you thanking me?” Xie Duan, still puzzled, thought the neighbor did not understand his meaning, yet the situation persisted. Later, when he asked more directly, the neighbor laughed and said, “You have already taken a wife, who is secretly cooking in the room, and you were saying I was cooking for you?” Xie Duan remained silent, puzzled, and did not know the reason.

After the rooster crowed, he went out early in the morning, and secretly returned before dawn. From outside the fence, he peeped into his home and saw a young girl coming out of a jar, heading to the stove to light a fire. Duan entered the door, directly went to the jar to look at the snails, but instead saw the girl. He then went to the stove and asked her, saying, "Where did the new bride come from, and why are you helping me cook?" The girl was greatly frightened and confused, wanting to return to the jar but unable to go back. She answered, "I am the White Water Maiden from the Milky Way. The Heavenly Emperor pitied you for being an orphan, and you have been respectful and cautious in your behavior, so He sent me to temporarily take care of the house and cook. In ten years, He will make you live in wealth and find a wife, and I will then return. But you have no reason to secretly peek at me. My form has been seen, so I should not stay any longer. I will leave you. However, after I leave, your situation will improve slightly. Work diligently in farming, fishing, and making a living. Leave this shell with you to store rice and grain; you will never lack for food." Duan begged her to stay, but she refused. At that time, suddenly a storm came, and she disappeared. Duan built an altar for her and made seasonal sacrifices. He usually had enough, but he did not become extremely wealthy. Then the villagers arranged for a wife for him. Later, he was appointed as a county magistrate. It is said that the White Water Maiden temple on the road is where this happened.

During the Tai Kang era of the Jin dynasty, there was a monk named Zhi Tan Sui from the Xie family. He was in his twenties, fair-skinned and handsome, and followed the popular practices of Buddhism. He often passed by the Qingxi Temple and once entered it to take a look. In the evening, he returned home and dreamed of a woman who came to him and said, "You will come to be my temple god and will not enter again." Zhi Tan Sui asked in his dream, "Who is the woman?" The woman replied, "I am the aunt of the Qingxi Temple." After about a month, he fell ill. On his deathbed, he told his young companions, "I have no great fortune, nor great sin, but after I die, I will become the god of the Qingxi Temple. When you pass by, look at me." After he died, the young monks went to the temple. Upon arriving, the spirit spoke in a spiritual voice to greet and inquire about each other, and her voice was as it was in the past. As she was leaving, she said, "I haven't heard the chanting for a long time, I want to hear it once more." His companion Hui Jin then performed the chanting. The spirit still sang praises. She said, "Parting on different paths, even now there is sorrow. How much more so for this strange phenomenon, the separation of form and spirit. The lament of the dark and mysterious, what can be said of such feelings." She then sobbed uncontrollably, and the monks all cried.

Wang Dao's son Yue was the Middle Book Attendant. Dao had a dream in which someone bought Yue for a million coins, and Dao secretly prepared for prayers. Soon after, he dug the ground and found a million coins. He felt very uneasy about it and hid them all one by one. When Yue's illness became severe, Dao was deeply worried and did not eat for several days. Suddenly, he saw a person with a very imposing appearance, wearing armor and holding a sword. He asked who the person was. The person replied, "I am Lord Jiang. Your son is not well, and I want to plead for his life, so I came here. Do not worry anymore." Dao then fed him, and he ate up to several measures. After eating, he suddenly said to Dao, "The Middle Book Attendant's fate is sealed, it cannot be saved." He disappeared after speaking, and Yue also died. In Dongye, Yun County, Kuaiji, there was a woman named Wu, whose courtesy name was Wangzi. While walking, she suddenly saw a noble person sitting upright and dignified, like Lord Jiang. He threw two tangerines to her. He appeared frequently, and their relationship became close. Whenever Wangzi had a desire, she would get it from the air. Whenever she thought of fish slices, a pair of carp would appear from the air.

During the rebellion led by Sun En, Wu County was in chaos, and a man suddenly rushed into the temple of Prince Jiang. As soon as he entered, a wooden statue bent its bow and shot an arrow at him, and he died immediately. Passersby and the temple guardians all witnessed this. During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, Gao Heng from Le'an served as the governor of Wei Commandery and garrisoned at Stone City. His grandson, Ya Zhi, was in the stable when he claimed that a spirit had descended upon him. The spirit introduced itself as "Old Man with White Hair," carrying a cane and shining with a radiant light. It took Ya Zhi on a mystical journey at night, arriving at Jingkou in the evening and returning. Later, Ya Zhi and his father were killed by Huan Xuan. During the reign of Emperor Yuan of Jin, a man from Yixing named Zhou traveled to the capital, riding a horse with two companions. He had not yet reached the village by evening. By the roadside, there was a small thatched house, and a young woman, about sixteen or seventeen years old, with a beautiful appearance and fresh, clean clothes, came out. She saw Zhou passing by and said, "It is already late, and the next village is still far away. How can you reach Linhe?" Zhou then asked to stay overnight. The woman prepared food for him. Around midnight, they heard a child calling "Ah Xiang," and the woman responded. Soon, she was told, "An official is summoning you to push the thunder chariot." The woman left, saying, "I have to go now." That night, there was a sudden and intense thunderstorm. In the morning, she returned. Zhou mounted his horse and looked at the place where he had stayed the night, finding only a new grave with traces of horse urine and remaining grass. Zhou was greatly surprised and regretful. Five years later, he indeed became the governor of Linhe.

A man from Yuzhang named Liu Guang, young and unmarried, went to a rural house where he met a woman who said, "I am the daughter of General He, who died at fourteen, and have been raised by the Queen Mother of the West, who has sent me to interact with people of the lower world." Liu Guang became intimate with her. She then said that she found a handkerchief under the mat, wrapped in camphor. Her mother took the handkerchief and burned it, revealing it to be fire-washed cloth. Grand Marshal Huan returned from Nan Zhou and visited the tomb of Emperor Jianwen. His attendants sensed that he had something unusual to say. When he got into the carriage, he told his attendants, "The late emperor has appeared to me in spirit." He did not recount what the emperor said, so no one knew. But when he was about to pay homage, he repeatedly said, "I dare not." He also asked his attendants about the appearance of Yin Juan. Someone replied, "Yin Juan was short and fat, dark-skinned, and very ugly." Huan said, "I also saw him standing beside the emperor, and he looked the same." He disliked him and then fell ill, dying shortly after.

● Chapter Six During the Han dynasty, a man from Juzhang County in Kuaiji traveled eastward and returned late in the evening. He had not yet reached his home and saw a small house by the roadside with a fire burning. He decided to stay there for the night. There was a young girl in the house who did not want to share the same room with the older man. She called a girl from a neighboring household to accompany her, and they played the qin together at night. When asked about her name, the girl did not answer directly but played the strings and sang: "Entwined vines on the gourd, one strand after another. If you wish to know my name, my surname is Chen and my given name is A Deng." The next morning, he arrived at the eastern outskirts of the city, where he found a woman selling food in a shop. He sat down to rest and told her about what he had seen the previous night. Upon hearing the name "A Deng," the woman was shocked and exclaimed, "That is my daughter, who recently passed away and was buried outside the city." In another story from the Han dynasty, a county official named Wu Xiang from Zhuji County grew weary of his duties and decided to flee to a deep mountain. He walked until he reached a stream, and as evening approached, he saw a young woman approaching. She was dressed very neatly. The woman said, "I live alone here, with no neighbors, only an elderly woman. She lives about ten steps away." Wu Xiang was pleased to hear this and followed her. After walking more than a mile, he arrived at her home, which was quite poor and humble. She prepared food for him. By the first watch of the night, he heard an old woman call out, "Zhang's granddaughter." The girl replied, "Yes." Wu Xiang asked who it was, and she answered, "It's the old woman we mentioned earlier." The two of them slept together. At dawn, when the rooster crowed, Wu Xiang left. They fell in love, and the girl gave him a purple handkerchief as a gift. Wu Xiang returned the favor with a cloth handkerchief. When they reached the place where she lived the previous day, they crossed the stream. That night, a sudden flood surged, too deep to cross. He returned to the girl's house but found no trace of where they had been, only a tomb remained.

Zhen Di Port in Lujiang is a place where a large ship once sank, said to be one of Cao Cao's vessels. Once, a fisherman stayed there overnight, mooring his boat nearby. He only heard the sounds of zither and flute, accompanied by a pervasive fragrance. In his dream, he was warned by someone to stay away from the official ship. Startled, he woke up and immediately moved his boat away. It is said that Cao Cao had several courtesans on board when the ship sank there, and the wreck still exists today. Lu Chong went hunting and shot a deer, hitting it. As he followed it, he didn't realize how far he had gone. Suddenly, he saw a gate resembling an official residence. He asked the gatekeeper, who replied, "This is the residence of Cao the Magistrate." Lu Chong then met Cao, who told him, "Your father has arranged a marriage for my daughter, so I have come to fetch you." Three days later, the wedding was over, and Cao sent Lu Chong back home in a carriage. When Lu's mother asked him about it, he gave a detailed account. After parting from Cao, four years later on the Double Third Festival, Lu Chong went to play by the water. He saw a small carriage by the water and approached it. Inside, he found Cao's daughter with a three-year-old child, and their feelings remained as they were. She handed the child to Lu Chong, gave him water and a gold bracelet, and then parted ways.

Wang Boyang lived in Jingkou, and to the east of his residence was a large house, which was said to be the tomb of Lu Su. Wang Boyang's wife was the sister of Xi Jian, and after her death, Wang Boyang leveled the tomb and buried her there. Several years later, while sitting in the hall, Wang Boyang suddenly saw a noble person riding a sedan chair, accompanied by hundreds of attendants, and all the horses were as black as if bathed in iron. The noble person came straight and sat down, saying to Wang Boyang, "I am Lu Zijing, and my tomb has been here for about two hundred years. Why did you destroy my home?" He then looked at the people around him and said, "Why don't you raise your hand!" The attendants pulled Wang Boyang off the bed, and then hit him with the blade of the ring hundreds of times before leaving. He died immediately. After a long time, he revived, but the places where he was hit all broke out in sores and ulcers, and soon after, he died. Another account says that Wang Boyang died, and his son prepared the tomb, finding a lacquered coffin and moving it to the South Hill. At night, he dreamed that Lu Su was angry and said, "I will kill your father." Soon after, he dreamed again that Wang Boyang said, "Lu Su and I are fighting over the tomb. If you are not as good as me, you will not be able to return." Later, he saw several spots of blood on the cushion of the spirit table and suspected it was because of Lu Su. The tomb is now one li east of Changguang Bridge.

承俭是东莞人。他病逝后被安葬在本县境内，十年后的一个夜晚，他在梦中向县令诉说：“我是已故的承俭，现在被人劫持，您的府上请快来救我。”县令立即下令准备百人队伍，派人骑马前往他的坟墓。承俭说他已经快要出来了，这时天突然下起了大雾，对面看不见人，只能听到坟墓中棺材被打破的声音。有两个人站在坟墓上观望，但浓雾遮挡了视线，看不见有人过来。县令到达后，百人同时大声呼喊，捉住了墓中的三个人。墓上的两个人趁机逃走了。棺材没有损坏，县令立刻让人修复。当晚，县令又梦见承俭说：“那两个人虽然逃走了，但我都记得他们的样子：一个人脸上有青色的标记，像一片藿叶；另一个人的前两颗门牙断了。您只要根据这些特征去寻找，自然能找到他们。”县令按照他的建议去追捕，最终将他们捉拿归案。 荆州刺史殷仲堪，在他还是平民的时候，在丹徒突然梦见一个人，那人自称是上虞人，已经去世，尸体漂浮在江中，明天就会到达。他说：“您有救人的仁心，能否将我的尸体移到高而干燥的地方，这样我的遗骨也能得到您的恩惠。”第二天，殷仲堪与众人一起到江边，看见一具棺材随着水流漂来，漂到了殷仲堪坐的地方。他立即派人将棺材拉上来，发现棺材上的铭文与梦中所见一致。于是将棺材移到高处，用酒饭祭奠。当晚，他又梦见这个人来感谢他。

During the Jingping era, the governor of Xuzhou, Suo Xun, was traveling by boat to Jinling. Suddenly, darkness fell, and they returned to the river, traveling several miles. A man asked to be carried, saying, "My home is at Han's tomb, and my feet hurt so I cannot walk; please take me in your boat." At the fourth watch of the night, they arrived at Han's tomb, and the man then left. Xun sent someone to pull the boat, crossing a ford, but applying force was very inconvenient. He scolded the man, saying, "I carried you several miles, and you just left, without helping to pull the boat." He wanted to hit him. The man then returned to help pull, and without realizing it, they managed to cross. The man then directly entered the area between the tombs. Xun suspected it was not a human and sent someone to secretly investigate. The man passed through the area between the tombs and was no longer seen. After a short while, he reappeared and went to a tomb, calling out, "Zaigong." Someone came out in response. The man said, "I came here in your boat, and you didn't help pull, so you wanted to hit me. Now I will go to report this, and I want to temporarily borrow Ganro." Zaigong said, "Destroying my Ganro is not possible." The man replied, "It doesn't hurt, I was just testing it." Xun heard this and immediately returned to the boat. After a short while, something came from the shore, red like a hundred斛龠, about two zhang long, and came straight towards the boat. Xun shouted loudly, "You carried me in your boat, and you didn't help pull, so you can't hit me! It is convenient to carry you, but Ganro now wants to hit me. Today, I will destroy your Ganro." After he finished speaking, it suddenly disappeared, and so he proceeded.

During the reign of Emperor Gong in the Eastern Jin Dynasty (420), Feng Shu of Shangdang served as an official in the Chancellor's office. He was going on leave to return to Huoluo. Suddenly, he encountered four people, each holding a rope and a staff, who came to meet him. Feng Shu spurred his horse to avoid them, but the horse refused to move forward. The four people each grabbed one of the horse's legs and suddenly arrived at the riverbank. They asked, "Do you want to cross?" Feng Shu replied, "The water is deep and unfathomable, and there are no boats. How can we cross? You are just going to kill us." The four people said, "We won't kill you; we will take you to the authorities." They then grabbed the horse's legs and crossed the river to the north. Feng Shu only heard the sound of waves but did not feel the water. Nearly reaching the shore, the four people talked to each other, saying, "This person is not clean. How can we take him away?" At that time, Feng Shu had a brother in mourning attire, and he was deeply afraid that the ghosts would leave him, fearing he would drown and die. He then whipped the horse to make it run and managed to reach the shore directly. He then thanked them, saying, "Having received your kindness, how dare I trouble you again."

Lord Wang Rong of Anfeng, courtesy name Junchong, was from Linyi, Langya. Once, he attended a funeral at someone's home. The host was still in the process of preparing the coffin, and all the mourners had entered the main hall. Lord Wang lay down in his carriage. Suddenly, he saw an unusual object in the sky, like a bird. Upon closer inspection, it grew larger and came closer. He saw a red horse-drawn carriage with a man inside, wearing a headband and red clothing, holding an axe. The man got out of the carriage and directly entered Lord Wang's carriage. He turned and accommodated himself in the carriage, saying to Lord Wang, "Your divine clarity and insight are such that nothing is hidden from you. I have something to say, so I have come to accompany you. I will give you one piece of advice: in general, when people are buried and mourned, if they are not close relatives, you should not rush to attend. If you have no choice, you can ride in a red carriage, let a bearded servant drive it, or ride a white horse, then you can ward off evil." He then told Lord Wang, "You will attain the position of one of the Three Dukes." They spoke for a long time. When the host placed the coffin inside for burial, all the guests entered, and this ghost also entered. After entering the house, the ghost took the axe and struck it on the wall of the coffin. One relative rushed to the coffin, wanting to bid farewell to the deceased. The ghost struck him on the forehead with the axe, and he immediately fell to the ground. The attendants helped him out. The ghost stood on the coffin, looked at Lord Wang, and laughed. Everyone saw the ghost holding the axe leave.

Li Ziyu, young and skilled in medical formulas, was praised by contemporary people for his supernatural insight. Xu Yong, serving as the governor of the state of Yu, was stationed in Liyang. His younger brother had suffered from chest and abdominal pain for over ten years and was nearly dead. One night, he suddenly heard a ghost behind the screen saying to the ghost in his abdomen, "Why not kill it quickly? Otherwise, Li Ziyu will pass by here. With the red pill, I will hit you, and you will die." The ghost in the abdomen responded, "I do not fear it." By dawn, Xu Yong then sent someone to wait for Ziyu, who indeed came. Not yet having entered the door, the patient heard groaning sounds inside. By the time Ziyu entered to examine, he said, "It is a ghostly illness." He then took out the Eight Toxins Red Pills from the medicine box and gave them to take. In a moment, thundering and drumming sounds in the abdomen, a lot of diarrhea several times, and finally recovered. Now the formula for the Eight Toxins Pills is this.

During the 14th year of the Yungang era of the Song dynasty, a man named Sheng Dao'er from Guangling died, leaving behind an orphaned daughter whom he entrusted to his brother-in-law, Shen Yizhi. After completing the mourning rites, Shen Yizhi arranged for his daughter to marry a man named Yan Qixi from the northern suburbs, who came from a low-ranking family. He lavished gifts and hospitality, and the marriage was finally consummated. Suddenly, Sheng Dao'er appeared from the air and angrily said, "With my very breath, I entrusted the future of our family to you. How could you forget righteousness for the sake of gain and marry into a lowly family?" Shen Yizhi was greatly frightened and ashamed. During the Jin dynasty, a man named Hu Maohui from Huainan had the ability to see ghosts. Although he did not like seeing them, he could not stop it. Later, he traveled to Yangzhou and returned to Lixia. In the east of the city, there was a shrine where a local was about to perform a ritual with a shaman. After a short while, a group of ghosts shouted at each other, "The superior official has arrived." They all fled out of the shrine. Looking back, they saw two monks enter the shrine. The ghosts, in twos and threes, embraced each other and hid in the grass near the shrine, watching and waiting. When they saw the monks, they were all filled with fear. After a short while, when the two monks left, the ghosts returned to the shrine. Hu Maohui thus came to believe in Buddhism and sincerely worshiped.

A rustic child, while grazing cattle in the wild, was accompanied by several friends. They saw a ghost leaning among the grass, setting traps everywhere with the intention of catching people. Before the ghost had finished setting the traps, the child secretly took the front trap and used it to catch the ghost, thereby capturing the ghost. Du Qian of Lujiang served as the magistrate of Zhujin. In the west of the county, there was a ghost three zhang long, wearing ochre-colored pants and a robe, waving and flapping in the grass. The ghost also took off the robe and threw it on the grass, singing a song of annoyance. The people all watched it. Zhu Bi of Kuaiji served as a court official. He was building his residence but died before it was completed. His fellow townsman Xie Zimu took over his affairs. Taking advantage of Zhu Bi's death, Xie Zimu falsified records to claim many rewards, amounting to over a million coins, and used this to defame Zhu Bi. In reality, the money was for himself. One night, while sleeping, Xie Zimu suddenly heard someone mentioning Zhu Bi's name. Soon after, the person appeared before Xie Zimu's hall and said, "You can defame a dead man, but you will face me on a certain day and night for a confrontation." After speaking, the person disappeared suddenly. Xia Houzong served as a military officer in the Western Command. He often saw ghosts riding horses filling the road, looking no different from people. Once, while traveling with someone, he was suddenly pulled to talk by a ghost, who pointed to a child on the road and said, "This child needs to fall ill." Soon after, the child indeed fell ill and nearly died. The child's mother heard about it and questioned Xia Houzong. Xia Houzong replied, "There is nothing to worry about. The child threw mud on the road and accidentally hit a ghost's foot. The ghost was angry and thus caused your child to fall ill. If you give the ghost some food and wine, it will recover." The mother followed his advice, and the child recovered.

Fan Qi from Shunyang was preparing to bury his mother. His mother's grave was in Shunyang, and he went to see it. Upon arrival, he found the graves to be numerous and disorderly, making it difficult to identify which one was his mother's. He did not know which grave was hers. At that time, Yuan Yanren was the governor of Yuzhou. He went to see Fan Qi and said, "I have heard of someone who can see ghosts." Fan Qi immediately followed his advice and had someone search for the grave. By the time they arrived, they reported, "There is a person in the tomb with such clothing and appearance." They immediately opened the tomb and found that all the coffin contents were rotten. The grave was filled with ash and soil to a depth of more than a foot. Fan Qi was very suspicious. He tried to have someone use their foot to stir the soil in the ash, hoping to find some old object. Indeed, they found a brick, and the inscription read "wife of Fan Jian." Then, he believed it. Monk Zhi, a monk from Kuaiji, had a very close relationship with Wang Tan, the Northern Attendant. They often discussed matters of life and death, karma, and retribution, which were vague and hard to understand. Therefore, they made a pact: if one of them died first, they would inform the other. After several years, Wang suddenly saw the monk come in the temple and said, "I died on a certain day of a certain month. Karma and retribution are not in vain; they respond like echoes. You should diligently cultivate virtue and morality to ascend to the divine. As we agreed earlier, the one who dies first will inform the other, so I came to tell you." After speaking, he suddenly disappeared. Wang soon died as well.

A man from Yue'an named Liu Chigou lived in Xia Kou. Suddenly, a ghost came to reside in Liu's house. At first, the ghost appeared vaguely as a human shape, wearing white cloth trousers. After that, it came every few days and no longer remained invisible. It would not leave and liked to steal food. The family did not consider it a problem, but they found it difficult to deal with. Initially, they dared not scold it. Ji Yizi, a strong and unafraid man who did not believe in ghosts, went to Liu's house and told the host, "Where is the ghost in your house? I will curse it for you." Immediately, there was a sound from the beam. There were many guests present, and they all looked up. The ghost then threw something down in a flurry, which hit Ji Yizi's face. He looked at it and saw that it was the undergarment of the host's wife, still dirty. Everyone laughed and had fun. Ji Yizi was greatly embarrassed, washed his face, and left. Someone told Liu, "This ghost steals food and eats everything. It must be a tangible object, and you can trap it with poison." Liu then cooked the herb *Tianma* in another house, took two cups of juice, and secretly brought it back home. Toward night, the whole family made porridge, and there was one bowl of leftover. Liu poured the *Tianma* juice into it, placed it on the table, and covered it with a bowl. After people settled down, they heard the ghost coming from outside. It opened the bowl and ate the porridge. After finishing, it threw the broken bowl away and left. In a short while, it vomited in the house, extremely angry, and pounded the windows. Liu had already prepared, and they fought back. The ghost also dared not enter. Until the fourth watch, it finally died.

**Chapter Seven** In the village of Baqiu,庐陵, there lived a man named Chen Ji, who served as a state official. His wife, Qin, lived alone at home. One day, a man appeared, over ten feet tall, with dignified and proper appearance, wearing a red and green robe, whose colors were dazzling. He came to visit her. Later, they would meet regularly at a mountain stream. They engaged in intimate relations without realizing it. This continued for several years. When neighbors entered the place where these events occurred, they would see a rainbow. Qin went to the water side, and the man used a golden bottle to draw water for them to drink. Afterward, she became pregnant and gave birth to a child who looked human but was fat. When Chen Ji returned temporarily, Qin feared seeing him, so she hid the child in a jar. This man gave her a golden bottle to cover the child and said, "This child cannot be taken away yet. You do not need to make clothes; I will clothe the child." He then wrapped the child in a red bag and allowed it to come out for feeding. During dark and stormy weather, neighbors saw a rainbow descending into her courtyard, transforming into a man, who took the child away, also during dark and stormy weather. People saw two rainbows coming out of her house. After several years, the man came back to visit the mother. Later, Qin went to the fields and saw two rainbows in the stream, feeling afraid. Shortly, she saw the man, who reassured her, "It is I, there is no need to be afraid." From then on, the supernatural visits ceased.

In the early years of the reign of Emperor Wu of Song, a man named Wang from Fuyang set up a crab trap in a small stream. One morning, he went to check the trap and found a piece of wood about two feet long inside it. The trap had broken, and all the crabs had escaped. He repaired the trap and removed the wood to the bank. The next morning, he checked again and found the same piece of wood back in the trap, with the trap broken again. He repaired the trap and removed the wood once more. The following morning, he saw the same situation, with the wood and the broken trap as before. Wang suspected that the wood was supernatural and decided to take it home. He placed it in a crab cage, tied it up, and carried it back, saying, "When I get home, I will chop it up and burn it." As he was about two or three miles from home, he heard a noise coming from the cage. He turned around and saw that the wood had transformed into a creature with a human face and a monkey's body, standing on one leg. The creature spoke to Wang, saying, "I have a fondness for crabs. Yesterday, I broke your crab trap and ate all the crabs inside. I have wronged you, and I hope you will forgive me. Open the cage and let me go. I am a mountain deity, and I will protect you and help you catch more crabs." Wang replied, "You are such a violent creature, and you have done this before. Your crimes deserve death." The creature pleaded earnestly, begging Wang to release it. Wang ignored it and continued walking. The creature then asked, "What is your name? I want to know it." It repeated the question, but Wang did not answer. As they got closer to his home, the creature said, "If you won't release me and won't tell me your name, what else can I do? I will just have to die." When Wang reached home, he set the creature on fire. Afterward, there was no more sound. Local folklore refers to this creature as a "shan yu," a creature that can harm people if it knows their names. That is why it repeatedly asked Wang for his name, intending to harm him to save itself.

In the first month of the first year of the false reign title "Jianyuan" used by Liu Cong, an earthquake struck Pingyang, causing the Chongming Pavilion to collapse into a pool. The water turned red like blood, and red vapor rose to the sky, followed by a red dragon leaping and leaving. A meteor started from the constellation of Ox (Niu) and entered the Purple Palace (Ziwei), taking on a winding dragon-like shape and illuminating the ground before falling ten miles north of Pingyang. Upon inspection, it appeared as meat with a foul smell spreading throughout Pingyang, measuring thirty steps in length and twenty-seven steps in width. There was a continuous crying sound near the meat, day and night. It was said that Liu Cong's wife, Liu, gave birth to a snake and an animal, both of which caused harm and fled. They were not found. Soon after, they appeared next to the fallen meat. Shortly thereafter, Liu died, and the crying sound ceased. After the revival of the Jin dynasty, Zhou Ziwén from Qiao County, whose home was in Jinling, enjoyed hunting as a youth. One day, while in the mountains, he suddenly saw a tall figure in a mountain ravine, about five or six zhang tall, holding a bow and arrow with an arrowhead about two feet wide, white like frost or snow. The figure suddenly called out, "Ah, rat." Zhou Ziwén unconsciously responded, "Yes." The figure then pulled the bowstring and aimed the arrow at Zhou Ziwén, causing him to lose his soul and collapse.

During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, a man named Qin Jing from Xuancheng often went to the Wuchang Mountains to pick tea leaves. One day, he suddenly encountered a tall, hairy figure, about ten feet tall, with hair covering his entire body, coming from the north of the mountain. Qin Jing was terrified and thought he would surely die. The hairy figure grabbed his arm and led him to a bend in the mountain, into a dense tea bush, and then left. Qin Jing continued to pick tea leaves. Shortly after, the figure returned and gave him twenty oranges from his pocket, which were unusually sweet and delicious. Qin Jing was very surprised and carried the tea leaves back home. In the early morning, Sheng Yi from Kuaiji often rose early and found no one on the road. Suddenly, he saw a person dressed in red clothing and a red hat, licking dew from the leaves on a willow tree outside his house. After a while, he suddenly saw Sheng Yi and became startled, then disappeared immediately. In the year 422 AD during the Song dynasty, a servant girl in the household of Xie Nankang was walking and encountered a black dog. The dog told her, "Look at what is behind me." She looked up and saw a person three feet tall with two heads. The servant girl was terrified and ran away, and the person and the dog followed her to her home. The entire family fled. The servant girl asked the dog, "What are you doing here?" The dog replied, "I just want to eat." The servant girl then prepared food for the dog. After they had eaten, the two-headed person appeared. The servant girl told the dog, "The person has already left." The dog said, "He is about to return." After a while, the figure disappeared. No one knew where he went. Later, the family suffered almost complete deaths.

Li Yi of Song County, whose father did not believe in ghosts and spirits, lived in a house that was originally considered so unlucky that anyone who lived there died. His father bought and moved into the house. For many years, they lived peacefully, and his descendants flourished. Li Yi became a high-ranking official, and when he was about to move his family to serve as an official, he gathered his relatives inside and outside the family. After the wine and food were served, his father said, "Is there really good and evil in the world? This house has always been said to be unlucky, but since I moved in, it has been peaceful and prosperous for many years, and I have been promoted. Where are the ghosts? From now on, it will be a lucky house. Those who live here will have no doubts." After finishing speaking, he went to the toilet. In a moment, he saw something in the wall, about the size of a rolled mat, about five feet tall, pure white. He immediately took a knife and stabbed it. It split in half, transforming into two people. He then cut it horizontally, and it turned into four people. He seized them and struck Li back, killing him. He carried them to the seat and killed his children. All who were surnamed Li would die, while others would not be harmed. Li Yi was still young, being held in someone's arms. The family knew of the change. The wet nurse carried him out the back door and hid him in another house, and only he escaped. Li Yi, whose courtesy name was Jingzhen, became the governor of Xiangdong.

During the reign of Emperor Song, Wang Zhongwen served as a minor official in the Henan Prefecture, residing north of Gou County. While on leave, he went walking in the wetlands at dusk. He saw a white dog behind his carriage and greatly loved it. He wanted to take it, but suddenly it transformed into a human-like form, resembling the Fangxiang, with eyes red like fire, showing teeth and sticking out its tongue, and was very repulsive. Wang Zhongwen was greatly terrified and, together with his servant, attacked it, but they could not withstand it and fled. He informed his family, and over ten people gathered, holding knives and carrying lights, and came to see it, but they did not know where it was. More than a month later, Wang Zhongwen suddenly saw it again. With his servant, he ran together, but before reaching home, they both fell to the ground and died.

● Chapter Eight Wáng Jī, the governor of Guangzhou, once entered the latrine and suddenly saw two people dressed in black clothing fighting with him. After a long struggle, he captured them and obtained two objects that resembled black ducks. He asked Bào Liàng about them, and Bào Liàng said, "These things are ominous." Wáng Jī burned them, and they flew straight up into the sky. Soon after, he was executed. During the Yìxī period of the Eastern Jin Dynasty, a man named Gě Huīfū from Wūshāng stayed at his wife's home. After the third watch of the night, two people carrying torches appeared at the front of the steps. Suspecting them to be evil people, Gě Huīfū went to attack them. As he was about to strike, they all transformed into butterflies, flying away in a colorful and scattered manner. One of the butterflies flew into Gě Huīfū's armpit, causing him to fall to the ground and die shortly after. After Zhūgě Chángmín became wealthy and powerful, he often experienced several nights each month where he would wake up in a panic, jumping around as if fighting with someone. Máo Xiūzhī, who had once stayed with him, witnessed this and was startled. He asked Zhūgě Chángmín why this happened, and Zhūgě Chángmín replied, "I just saw something very black with fur, whose feet were not clearly visible, and it was strangely powerful. No one else could control it." Later, strange occurrences became frequent in the house, with snake heads appearing in the pillars and rafters. When someone tried to cut them with a blade, they would disappear but reappear immediately. The washing sticks also seemed to talk to each other in a human-like voice, though the words were incomprehensible. Gě Huīfū also saw giant hands on the walls, each about seven or eight feet long and with arms as thick as several encirclements. When he tried to cut them, they suddenly vanished. Not long after, Zhūgě Chángmín was executed.

Yu Jin of Xinye, whose mother was ill, had three brothers who were all attending to her. One night, they lit a fire, and suddenly saw the curtain rope rolling up and down by itself, doing so several times. Soon after, they heard an unusual dog sound at the foot of the bed. The whole family looked around, but saw no dog, only a dead human head on the ground, with hair still on it and the eyes moving, which was extremely terrifying. The family was filled with fear.
